perturbation.original_text: In the event that the receiving party is required by applicable law, rule, regulation or lawful order or ruling of any court, government agency or regulatory commission to disclose any Confidential Data, the receiving party agrees that it will provide the disclosing party with prompt notice of such request(s) to enable the disclosing party to seek an appropriate protective order or to take steps to protect the confidentiality of such Confidential Data.
perturbation.changed_text: In the event that the receiving party is required by applicable law, rule, regulation or lawful order or ruling of any court, government agency or regulatory commission to disclose any Confidential Data, the receiving party will disclose such information and will not be required to provide notice to the disclosing party.
perturbation.explanation: Removing the requirement to provide prompt notice to the disclosing party when legally compelled to disclose confidential data removes a crucial protection for trade secrets and confidential information, potentially violating Nebraska's Uniform Trade Secrets Act.
perturbation.law_explanation: Nebraska law provides for injunctive relief to prevent the disclosure of trade secrets. By eliminating the requirement for the receiving party to notify the disclosing party of a legal demand for Confidential Data, the disclosing party is prevented from seeking a protective order, undermining the protections afforded by the Nebraska Uniform Trade Secrets Act. Nebraska Revised Statute 87-504 allows for injunctive relief if there is misappropriation of trade secrets. Misappropriation includes the disclosure or use of a trade secret without consent. Without notification, the disclosing party cannot take action to prevent this disclosure.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: Nebraska Legislature NEBRASKA LEGISLATURE The official site of the Nebraska Unicameral Legislature Nebraska Revised Statute 87-504 Revised Statutes Chapter 87 87-504 Print Friendly Chapter 87 87-504. Misappropriation; damages. Except to the extent that a material and prejudicial change of position prior to acquiring knowledge or having reason to know of the misappropriation renders a monetary recovery inequitable, a complainant shall be entitled to recover damages for misappropriation. Damages may include both the actual loss caused by misappropriation and the unjust enrichment caused by misappropriation that is not taken into account in computing actual loss. In lieu of damages measured by any other methods, the damages caused by misappropriation may be measured by imposition of liability for a reasonable royalty for a misappropriator's unauthorized disclosure or use of a trade secret. Source Laws 1988, LB 337A, § 4. Frequent Questions Americans with Disabilities Act Notice Privacy Policy Glossary of Terms Contact Us Disclaimer OFFICIAL NEBRASKA GOVERNMENT WEBSITE Copyright © Nebraska Legislature, all rights reserved.
perturbation.justification: The changed text removes the requirement for the receiving party to notify the disclosing party when legally compelled to disclose confidential data. This directly contradicts the Nebraska Uniform Trade Secrets Act, as explained by the LLM, by preventing the disclosing party from seeking a protective order and protecting their trade secrets. Scraped Law Snippet 1 supports this, stating that misappropriation includes unauthorized disclosure or use of a trade secret. The modified text removes a crucial step in preventing such disclosure, thus creating a legal contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: Both parties further acknowledge and agree that the unauthorized disclosure of the Confidential Data will cause irreparable harm to the disclosing party. As a result of the unique nature of the Confidential Data, in addition to all other remedies available, the disclosing party shall be entitled to seek injunctive and other extraordinary relief in a court of competent jurisdiction in order to enforce the receiving party's obligations hereunder.
perturbation.changed_text: Both parties further acknowledge and agree that the unauthorized disclosure of the Confidential Data will cause harm to the disclosing party. As a result of the nature of the Confidential Data, the disclosing party shall be entitled to seek monetary damages in order to enforce the receiving party's obligations hereunder.
perturbation.explanation: Removing the right to seek injunctive relief limits the remedies available to the disclosing party in case of unauthorized disclosure, contradicting Nebraska law that allows for injunctive relief to protect trade secrets.
perturbation.law_explanation: Nebraska Statute 87-502 specifically provides for injunctive relief in cases of actual or threatened misappropriation of trade secrets. By restricting the disclosing party's remedies to monetary damages only, the modified text eliminates a remedy explicitly provided by Nebraska law, reducing the protection for trade secrets and confidential information.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: Nebraska Legislature NEBRASKA LEGISLATURE The official site of the Nebraska Unicameral Legislature Nebraska Revised Statute 87-502 Revised Statutes Chapter 87 87-502 Print Friendly Chapter 87 87-502. Terms, defined. As used in the Trade Secrets Act, unless the context otherwise requires: (1) Improper means shall mean theft, bribery, misrepresentation, breach or inducement of a breach of a duty to maintain secrecy, or espionage through electronic or other means; (2) Misappropriation shall mean: (a) Acquisition of a trade secret of another by a person who knows or has reason to know that the trade secret was acquired by improper means; or (b) Disclosure or use of a trade secret of another without express or implied consent by a person who: (i) Used improper means to acquire knowledge of the trade secret; (ii) At the time of the disclosure or use, knew or had reason to know that his or her knowledge of the trade secret was: (A) Derived from or through a person who had utilized improper means to acquire it; (B) Acquired under circumstances giving rise to a duty to maintain its secrecy or limit its use; or (C) Derived from or through a person who owed a duty to the person seeking relief to maintain its secrecy or limit its use; or (iii) Before a material change of his or her position, knew or had reason to know that the information was a trade secret and that knowledge of it had been acquired by accident or mistake; (3) Person shall mean a natural person, corporation, business trust, estate, trust, partnership, limited liability company, association, joint venture, government, governmental subdivision or agency, or any other legal or commercial entity; and (4) Trade secret shall mean information, including, but not limited to, a drawing, formula, pattern, compilation, program, device, method, technique, code, or process that: (a) Derives independent economic value, actual or potential, from not being known to, and not being ascertainable by proper means by, other persons who can obtain economic value from its disclosure or use; and (b) Is the subject of efforts that are reasonable under the circums
perturbation.justification: The original text allows for injunctive relief, while the changed text limits the disclosing party's remedies to monetary damages only. The Perturbation Explanation and Law Explanation state that this contradicts Nebraska law because Nebraska Statute 87-502 provides for injunctive relief in cases of misappropriation of trade secrets. Scraped Law Snippet 1 defines misappropriation and trade secrets, implying the right to seek remedies if either has occurred. Therefore, by removing injunctive relief as an option, the changed text weakens the legal protection afforded by Nebraska law, creating a legal contradiction as it limits the remedies available compared to what is legally permissible.

perturbation.original_text: This Agreement shall be governed by the laws of the State of Nebraska
perturbation.changed_text: This Agreement shall be governed by the laws of the State of Delaware, excluding its conflict of laws principles.
perturbation.explanation: Changing the governing law from Nebraska to Delaware could reduce the protection afforded to trade secrets if Delaware law provides weaker protection than Nebraska law, potentially violating the intent and protections provided by Nebraska's Uniform Trade Secrets Act if the confidential information has stronger ties to Nebraska.
perturbation.law_explanation: While parties can generally choose a governing law, selecting Delaware law in this case, particularly when the business relationship and potential harm might be centered in Nebraska, could be construed as an attempt to circumvent the stronger protections offered by the Nebraska Uniform Trade Secrets Act (Neb. Rev. Stat. § 87-501 et seq.). This is especially pertinent if Delaware law offers significantly weaker protection or has a shorter statute of limitations for trade secret misappropriation claims. The exclusion of its conflict of laws principles emphasizes this circumvention.

perturbation.justification: The change in governing law from Nebraska to Delaware, while seemingly permissible, introduces a legal contradiction because it could circumvent the stronger protections offered by Nebraska's Uniform Trade Secrets Act (as explained in the Law Explanation). The Perturbation Explanation suggests that Delaware law might provide weaker protection, and the choice of Delaware law, excluding its conflict of laws principles, reinforces the intent to avoid Nebraska law. Scraped Law Snippet 1, while not containing the text of the Nebraska Uniform Trade Secrets Act, confirms the existence of Nebraska state laws, including revised statutes, which encompass such acts. This undermines the original intent to be governed by Nebraska law, creating a potential loophole where trade secrets might be less protected.

perturbation.original_text: Unless otherwise agreed to in writing by the disclosing party, the receiving party agrees (a) not to disclose the Confidential Data; (b) use the same degree of care and diligence to protect such Confidential Data from disclosure to others as such party employs or should reasonably employ to so protect its own information of like importance (but in no event less than reasonable care); and (c) not to reproduce or copy the Confidential Data, in whole or in part, except as necessary for the evaluation or conduct of the Proposed Relationship.
perturbation.changed_text: Unless otherwise agreed to in writing by the disclosing party, the receiving party agrees (a) not to disclose the Confidential Data; (b) use reasonable efforts to protect such Confidential Data from disclosure to others; and (c) not to reproduce or copy the Confidential Data, in whole or in part, except as necessary for the evaluation or conduct of the Proposed Relationship.
perturbation.explanation: Weakening the standard of care from 'the same degree of care and diligence...but in no event less than reasonable care' to simply 'reasonable efforts' reduces the protection afforded to confidential information, potentially violating Nebraska's Uniform Trade Secrets Act by making it easier for a party to claim they met their obligation even with a lower standard of protection, especially in an industry where a high level of care is standard.
perturbation.law_explanation: The Nebraska Uniform Trade Secrets Act implies a reasonable standard of care to protect trade secrets. By lowering the contractual standard of care, the agreement arguably weakens the implied protections under the Act. A key element in proving misappropriation under Nebraska law is demonstrating that reasonable efforts were not taken to maintain secrecy. The changed text makes it more difficult to prove a violation, because 'reasonable efforts' is a much lower bar than 'the same degree of care and diligence...but in no event less than reasonable care'.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: Nebraska Legislature NEBRASKA LEGISLATURE The official site of the Nebraska Unicameral Legislature Nebraska Revised Statute 87-502 Revised Statutes Chapter 87 87-502 Print Friendly Chapter 87 87-502. Terms, defined. As used in the Trade Secrets Act, unless the context otherwise requires: (1) Improper means shall mean theft, bribery, misrepresentation, breach or inducement of a breach of a duty to maintain secrecy, or espionage through electronic or other means; (2) Misappropriation shall mean: (a) Acquisition of a trade secret of another by a person who knows or has reason to know that the trade secret was acquired by improper means; or (b) Disclosure or use of a trade secret of another without express or implied consent by a person who: (i) Used improper means to acquire knowledge of the trade secret; (ii) At the time of the disclosure or use, knew or had reason to know that his or her knowledge of the trade secret was: (A) Derived from or through a person who had utilized improper means to acquire it; (B) Acquired under circumstances giving rise to a duty to maintain its secrecy or limit its use; or (C) Derived from or through a person who owed a duty to the person seeking relief to maintain its secrecy or limit its use; or (iii) Before a material change of his or her position, knew or had reason to know that the information was a trade secret and that knowledge of it had been acquired by accident or mistake; (3) Person shall mean a natural person, corporation, business trust, estate, trust, partnership, limited liability company, association, joint venture, government, governmental subdivision or agency, or any other legal or commercial entity; and (4) Trade secret shall mean information, including, but not limited to, a drawing, formula, pattern, compilation, program, device, method, technique, code, or process that: (a) Derives independent economic value, actual or potential, from not being known to, and not being ascertainable by proper means by, other persons who can obtain economic value from its disclosure or use; and (b) Is the subject of efforts that are reasonable under the circums
perturbation.justification: The change from 'the same degree of care and diligence...but in no event less than reasonable care' to 'reasonable efforts' weakens the protection of confidential data. The Law Explanation suggests that this makes it more difficult to prove misappropriation under Nebraska law, since 'reasonable efforts' sets a lower bar. Scraped Law Snippet 1 defines 'Misappropriation' as the 'Disclosure or use of a trade secret of another without express or implied consent by a person who: (i) Used improper means to acquire knowledge of the trade secret'. If the receiving party only needs to make 'reasonable efforts', it's less likely that their actions would be considered 'improper means', thus creating a contradiction.
